title: Choosing and storing livestock feed
subtitle: Sacks, bins, and the dry shed that keeps your feed bill down.
excerpt: A smallholding goes through a lot of feed sacks. Picking the right product per species and storing it so the rats and the damp do not get to it is the difference between a feed bill that works and one that creeps up every quarter. This covers the sack types, the storage kit, and the rotation rules that keep feed fresh.
sourceNotes: AHDB feed-storage best-practice notes. UFAS Universal Feed Assurance Scheme guidance. Smallholder Magazine feed-purchasing columns. SAC Consulting Scotland on-farm grain and feed storage advice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A toxic compound produced by moulds growing on damp grain or feed. Mycotoxins cause poor weight gain, abortion in breeding stock, and chronic ill-health. Dry storage prevents the mould that produces them.

Universal Feed Assurance Scheme, the UK industry quality standard for compound feeds. UFAS-assured feeds carry traceability through the supply chain and are required for stock sold into the food chain.

content.text: A smallholding running chickens, a few sheep, and a couple of pigs gets through a steady stream of feed sacks. Pick the right product per species, buy in volumes that get used before going stale, and store the sacks somewhere the rats and the damp cannot reach. The feed bill stays predictable and the stock stays in good condition.
Picking the right product

Each species has a standard complete feed designed for the stage of life. Hens get layers' pellets or mash. Pigs get sow-and-weaner nuts or finishing nuts. Sheep get ewe nuts or lamb creep pellets. Goats get goat mix or grower nuts. Rabbits get rabbit pellets. The bag tells you the protein percentage, the metabolisable energy, and the age range the feed is meant for. Picking the right product matters more than picking the cheapest sack.
Buy
UFAS

assured feeds whenever you can. The scheme guarantees traceability of the ingredients through the supply chain and is a requirement if you sell stock or meat into the food chain. Most feed merchants stock UFAS feeds as standard; check the bag corner for the logo.
Sack sizes and rotation

Compound feeds keep best within twelve weeks of milling; longer than that and the vitamin content starts to drop and the oils in the feed turn slightly rancid. The bag printing carries a use-by date about ninety days from milling. Buy sack sizes that suit your rate of use rather than buying in bulk for the sake of a discount. A flock of six hens gets through a twenty-kilo sack of layers' pellets in three to four weeks. A pen of two finisher pigs gets through a twenty-five-kilo sack of finishing nuts in four to five days.

Rotate stock so the oldest sack opens first. Mark the delivery date on each sack with a marker pen as soon as it comes in. Two sacks of the same feed waiting to be used is fine; ten sacks of the same feed waiting to be used means some of them will be past their best by the time they are opened.
The feed shed

Feed lives in a dry well-ventilated shed or barn corner away from the stock. Concrete or solid wood floor; a damp earthen floor wicks moisture into the bottom of any sack. A pallet under each row of sacks lifts them off the floor and lets air circulate. Sacks should not touch outside walls where condensation forms in winter. A simple workshop thermometer-and-hygrometer on the wall shows you whether the shed is dry enough; below sixty per cent humidity is the working aim.
Galvanised feed bins

Open sacks attract rats and mice. The standard fix is a galvanised metal bin with a heavy tight-fitting lid. The bin holds the contents of one sack and reseals automatically when the lid drops. A bin holds the feed dry, keeps rodents out, and lasts twenty years if kept under cover. Use one bin per feed type; mixing feeds in a single bin invites a wrong-feeding mistake when the bin gets low.

An old chest freezer with the cable cut serves the same role at a fraction of the price. The seal is good, the lid is heavy enough to defeat any rodent, and the shape suits stacking taller volumes. Plastic dustbins are not rodent-proof; a rat chews through plastic in days.
Rats, mice, and mould

Spilled feed brings rats. Sweep the floor of the feed shed every time you scoop a bag, and tip any spilled feed back into the bin or the chickens' bowl. A small rat colony moves in within a fortnight if there is loose feed on the floor. Check the perimeter of the shed for new holes or droppings on the morning round; deal with a small problem before it grows.
Damp sacks grow moulds that produce
mycotoxins

. A sack with a musty smell or visible white or green patches is binned (the dustbin, not the chicken bowl). The cost of one sack is small; the cost of a sow off her feed for a week from mycotoxin contamination is steep.
Buying habits that work

Most smallholdings settle on a regular delivery rhythm with one or two merchants. A monthly order, dropped at a known time, with a standard sack mix that covers the species kept, takes one job off the weekly planning. Buying ad-hoc means chasing sacks on bad weather days and running out at the wrong moment. The merchant lays the price for an account holder a touch lower than the over-the-counter price; it pays to set up an account.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Feed-store troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Most feed-store problems are damp, rodent, or rotation issues.

items.symptom: Sack of pig nuts has a musty smell when opened.
items.cause: Damp got into the storage, or the sack sat too long.
items.fix: Do not feed the contents. Check the use-by date and the storage conditions. Move the remaining sacks off any damp floor and on to pallets. Replace the bin or shed seal if water got in.

items.symptom: Hole in the corner of a sack with feed leaking out.
items.cause: Rats or mice have found the storage.
items.fix: Move all open sacks into sealed bins today. Sweep the shed and tip spilled feed into the bins. Check the shed for entry points and seal them. Consider a few traps along the wall.

items.symptom: Hens going off lay after a sack change.
items.cause: New batch may be a lower-protein formulation, or the previous sack was stale.
items.fix: Check the bag against the previous one; some merchants substitute brands silently. Hold the new sack for two weeks and watch lay rate. If it does not recover, go back to the previous brand.

items.symptom: Account at the merchant running higher than expected.
items.cause: Buying ad-hoc by the sack, paying retail rather than account price.
items.fix: Set up a monthly account delivery covering the full feed mix. Track the monthly spend over a quarter; the rate per kilo drops by ten to fifteen per cent for an account holder on a regular delivery.

content.text: A working feed shed is dry, rodent-proof, and stocked just ahead of need rather than weeks ahead. Set the bins up once, mark the delivery dates on every sack, and the daily feed routine becomes one of the easiest jobs on the holding.
